Article content: {"plain_text":"This is a piece of comms I shared internally at Sentry recently. Publishing it more broadly as I think the general sentiment is important for many pre-GPT companies.

How many times do you solve a problem without ever directly interacting with the source of that information? How many times do you Google something and have Gemini feed you information it’s parsed from websites, to the point where you don’t even bother clicking into any results? How many times, rather than using search, do you just open ChatGPT or Claude to get a quick pulse on something? I find myself doing it almost exclusively these days. These are just the most basic level agentic interactions that have already changed our behavior, and more are coming.
When it comes to Sentry, that means we need to consider how this changes our product and our business. That has led me to repeating something quite a lot lately, which usually means I need to be saying it with a megaphone rather than a telephone.
We need to be API-first.
While it’s true that Sentry has always been somewhat API-first (our API powers our UI), it’s never been a center point of our investments. I built our OAuth provider almost a decade ago, and until this moment in time, it never truly mattered. It was just me “completing the features” of a developer API, without there actually being demand for it. That has now changed, and importantly, it’s no longer enough for the API to simply exist. It now needs to be intentional.
MCP is a great example of the importance of these investments. We’re peaking at  <redacted> DAUs (vs. <6x MCP DAUs> on the web UI). It makes sense - it’s hugely beneficial having agents interact with our data when you’re working on fixing bugs. It doesn’t come for free, though - it’s not simply the agent consuming our UI, and it requires us to design these new interfaces for a different kind of customer. These new customers may not even ever interact with some parts of our product. It means once again building from first principles and considering how (and what of) these interactions need to exist.
That also means we have to recognize that the interaction with products at Sentry will no longer be exclusively through our web application, and in time it likely becomes a minority of customer usage. Our “customers” - if you will - have become Claude, Codex, and Cursor, as well as an ever-growing number of other agents. To design for those customers it starts with the API, and then translating that into great interfaces on top of it: MCP, CLI, the plugin ecosystem, and of course the web. That means putting more emphasis on the machine interaction points, treating them as equal counterparts to the human interaction points.
It requires us to really consider how folks interact with our product at all stages. It sounds simple at a high level: add support to the MCP and CLI. But the reality is it’s much more complicated than that. How do folks onboard to the product now? I, for one, tell my agent to go set up Sentry, and then I get quickly frustrated that it does it poorly. It does it poorly because it’s not a first-class interface we’ve built in Sentry. Those interfaces in many cases are now more impactful than the traditional web interface, and they're what is going to bring us our next 100,000 customers.
Related to this is an important sentiment that I’ve heard from teams like Anthropic: design for six months from now. We don’t know exactly what things will look like, but we can extrapolate with reasonable projections. Those projections show more agentic usage, not less, from even more sources. So I ask that everyone consider, for every product we work on, that we have a hard requirement for great API design, and that everything also includes a plan to support it across this next generation of customers using agents.","title":"Designing for Agents","preview_text":"This is a piece of comms I shared internally at Sentry recently. Publishing it more broadly as I think the general sentiment is important for many pre-GPT companies.
